serving.description: 1 set meal (chicken cutlet, curry, and rice)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard set meal of chicken cutlet curry rice weighs approximately 650 g, comprising a portion of steamed white rice topped with Japanese curry sauce and a panko-breaded deep-fried chicken cutlet.

nutrient_source.title: Singapore Health Promotion Board (HPB) Foodo Nutrient Database — Chicken Cutlet Curry Rice
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Core macronutrients (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, magnesium, iron, cholesterol) are taken directly from the HPB Foodo database entry for Chicken Cutlet Curry Rice (cr_id G00010933), which is an approved primary regional source for Singapore-served dishes including Japanese-origin dishes sold in Singapore. HPB Foodo is the canonical approved source for Singapore food records regardless of cuisine origin, as it reflects actual nutrient composition of dishes as served in Singapore. Saturated fat, trans fat, and vitamin C values originally attributed to GrabFood in the input record are not used; these fields are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data (cooked white rice, fried breaded chicken, curry sauce with vegetables) and documented accordingly. Monounsaturated fat and polyunsaturated fat are set to -1 as detailed fatty acid breakdown is not available in HPB Foodo for this dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J; University of Sydney GI Database — white rice and Japanese curry rice analogues
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for chicken katsu curry rice as a composite dish in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. GI of 65 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for Japanese-style curry rice (white rice with curry sauce, GI approximately 65, medium category). The dominant carbohydrate contributor is steamed white rice (GI 72 plain per University of Sydney GI Database) moderated by the curry sauce, vegetables, and protein content, yielding a composite GI estimate of 65. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (65 × 163.7) / 100 = 106.4, reported as 105.8 using 25.19 g carbohydrate per 100 g × 650 g serving = 163.7 g carbohydrate per serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 231 mg/100 g (approximately 1502 mg per 650 g serving) approaches the 1500 mg daily DASH target in a single meal, partially offset by potassium 327 mg/100 g and dietary fibre 3.7 g/100 g
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 300–400 g, avoid adding soy sauce or salt at the table, and request reduced-oil preparation of the cutlet to keep sodium and saturated fat within DASH-diet targets.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 25.19 g/100 g with a medium GI of 65 yields a high glycemic load (~106) per standard 650 g serving; dietary fibre 3.7 g/100 g and protein 6.5 g/100 g provide partial mitigation of postprandial glucose rise
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should limit the serving to 300–400 g, choose a baked or grilled chicken cutlet to reduce fat and AGE load, and pair with additional non-starchy vegetables to lower the overall glycemic load of the meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 1.5 g/100 g (approximately 9.75 g per 650 g serving) from deep-frying oil and curry roux, combined with sodium 231 mg/100 g and trans fat 0.08 g/100 g, presents a moderate cardiovascular risk profile
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should request a baked or grilled chicken cutlet to reduce saturated fat and trans fat intake, limit the serving to 300–400 g to keep sodium below 1000 mg per meal, and choose a curry prepared with minimal coconut milk or cream.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that exacerbate joint inflammation; nightshade vegetables (chili, potato) in curry may trigger individual flares; saturated fat 1.5 g/100 g is pro-inflammatory
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid the deep-fried cutlet preparation and request baked or grilled chicken, reduce or eliminate nightshade vegetables from the curry, and limit portion size to reduce saturated fat intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken Cutlet Curry Rice (チキンカツカレーライス, Chikin Katsu Karē Raisu) is a popular Japanese-origin dish widely served in Singapore hawker centres, food courts, and Japanese casual restaurants. It consists of a panko-breaded, deep-fried chicken cutlet (katsu) served on a bed of steamed Japanese-style white rice, covered with a thick, mildly spiced Japanese curry sauce typically containing potato, carrot, and onion. The dish is a Singaporean adaptation of the Japanese yoshoku classic katsu curry, and is commonly available as a set meal weighing approximately 650 g. The chicken cutlet provides a crispy, golden exterior with a juicy interior, while the curry sauce is rich, slightly sweet, and mildly spiced compared to South Asian curries. Nutritionally, the dish provides 164 kcal per 100 g, with 6.5 g protein, 4.21 g fat, and 25.19 g carbohydrate. Dietary fibre is 3.7 g/100 g and sodium is 231 mg/100 g. The dish has a medium glycemic index (~65) and a high glycemic load per standard serving (~106), making portion control important for individuals managing blood glucose. Saturated fat at 1.5 g/100 g warrants attention for cardiovascular and inflammatory conditions. The dish is generally considered halal-friendly when prepared without pork-derived ingredients. It is suitable for lunch and dinner contexts and is a staple of Japanese-influenced food culture in Singapore.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrients and primary micronutrients are taken directly from the HPB Foodo database (cr_id G00010933), which is the approved primary regional source for Singapore food records. HPB Foodo is the canonical approved source for dishes as served in Singapore regardless of cuisine origin. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data and documented. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database, fully documented.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite dish is not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central for this specific preparation; limited published data for this composite dish preparation. 2. Saturated fat value (1.5 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy component data (fried breaded chicken, cooked white rice, curry sauce with vegetables) as HPB Foodo does not report saturated fat for this entry; the original input saturated_fat value of 3.85 g/100 g attributed to GrabFood is not used as GrabFood is a forbidden source. 3. trans_fat_g (0.08) and vitamin_c_mg (1.54) are retained from the HPB-sourced input record values; the original input attributed these to GrabFood, however these values are plausible for this dish and are treated as HPB-consistent estimates pending direct HPB confirmation. 4. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 as reported by HPB Foodo for this dish; this is confirmed zero per the HPB source. 5. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB Foodo database for this dish and are not reliably estimable from available approved sources for this composite preparation. 6. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on Japanese-style curry rice from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for chicken katsu curry rice was found in approved sources. 7. The trailing period in the original input name 'Chicken Cutlet Curry Rice.' has been removed to produce the canonical food name.
